title: The Prism Manifold Realization Problem II
overall_summary: The paper classifies prism manifolds P(p,q) with q>p that arise by positive integral surgery on knots in S^3, using Donaldson theory, Heegaard Floer correction terms, and Greene's changemaker-lattice machinery. The core lattice-theoretic work compares a C-type plumbing lattice, with its vertex basis, to a changemaker lattice, with its standard basis, by classifying and controlling irreducible and unbreakable elements. The parts most useful for the original problem are Sections 3 and 4: they give interval descriptions of irreducible elements in a positive definite path-like plumbing lattice, formulas for pairings and norms of interval sums, criteria for breakability in terms of high-weight vertices, and graph-theoretic constraints on how irreducible elements can intersect. The paper also repeatedly uses the principle that irreducible basis elements cannot split across an orthogonal direct-sum decomposition, so connectedness of the pairing graph forces lattice indecomposability.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let C(p,q) be the C-type positive definite lattice with basis x0,x1,...,xn, pairings ⟨x0,x0⟩=4, ⟨xi,xi⟩=ai for i>0, ⟨x0,x1⟩=-2, ⟨xi,xi+1⟩=-1 for i>0, and all other basis pairings zero, where a1≥3 and ai≥2. For a subset I={xa,xa+1,...,xb} of consecutive basis elements, write [I]=xa+xa+1+...+xb and call [I] an interval. If v is an irreducible element of C(p,q), meaning v cannot be written v=x+y with nonzero x,y and ⟨x,y⟩≥0, then v=ε[I] for some ε in {1,-1} and some interval [I].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper does not prove this classification internally; it states that, because a1≥3, the result is immediate from Greene's Proposition 3.3 in [Gre13]. In the paper it is used as a black box converting arbitrary irreducible elements of the C-type lattice into signed sums over consecutive vertices of the distinguished path-like basis. This conversion is the gateway for all later combinatorial arguments, because pairings of irreducibles can then be computed by looking at overlaps and boundary edges of intervals.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the C-type lattice C(p,q) with vertex basis x0,...,xn and pairings as in Proposition 3.2, the lattice C(p,q) is indecomposable: it is not isomorphic to a direct orthogonal sum L1⊕L2 of two nonzero lattices.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume C(p,q)=L1⊕L2. The paper uses that each vertex basis element xi is irreducible. If xi has projections ai in L1 and bi in L2, then xi=ai+bi and ⟨ai,bi⟩=0; irreducibility forces one projection to vanish, so xi lies entirely in one summand. But adjacent vertex basis elements have nonzero pairing, namely ⟨x0,x1⟩=-2 and ⟨xi,xi+1⟩=-1 for i>0, so two adjacent xi cannot lie in different orthogonal summands. The pairing graph on the vertex basis is connected, hence all xi lie in the same summand and the other summand is trivial.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the C-type lattice C(p,q), for every lattice element v, the pairing ⟨x0,v⟩ is an even integer. Consequently the reflection rx0 defined by rx0(v)=v−2⟨x0,v⟩/⟨x0,x0⟩ x0 is an involution of the integral lattice C(p,q).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Write v=c0x0+c1x1+...+cnxn. Since x0 pairs only with x0 and x1, and those pairings are ⟨x0,x0⟩=4 and ⟨x0,x1⟩=-2, one has ⟨x0,v⟩=4c0−2c1, which is even. Therefore rx0(v)=v−(⟨x0,v⟩/2)x0 has integral coefficients. The usual reflection identity in a nonisotropic vector gives rx0(rx0(v))=v and preserves the form.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the C-type lattice C(p,q), call a vertex xi high weight if i>0 and |xi|=⟨xi,xi⟩=ai>2. An element ℓ is breakable if ℓ=x+y with |x|≥3, |y|≥3, and ⟨x,y⟩=-1; otherwise it is unbreakable. For any interval [I] and sign ε in {1,-1}, the signed interval ε[I] is unbreakable if and only if [I] contains at most one high-weight vertex.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The case I={x0} is immediate. If [I] does not contain x0, the statement is exactly the analogous result for linear lattices, cited as Greene's Corollary 3.5(4) in [Gre13]. If [I] contains x0, Lemma 3.5 gives the integral reflection rx0 across x0⊥. This reflection exchanges intervals with left endpoint 0 and intervals with left endpoint 1, preserves norms and pairings, and therefore preserves breakability. Thus the case of intervals containing x0 is reduced to intervals not containing x0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let [I] and [J] be intervals in the C-type lattice C(p,q). Let C be the graph with vertices x0,...,xn, two edges between x0 and x1, and one edge between xi and xi+1 for 0<i<n. An edge of C is called dangling for [I] and [J] if one endpoint lies in I, the other endpoint lies in J, and at least one endpoint is not in I∩J. Let δ([I],[J]) be the number of dangling edges. Then ⟨[I],[J]⟩=|[I∩J]|−δ([I],[J]), where |[I∩J]| means the norm of the sum of the common vertices.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Expand ⟨[I],[J]⟩ as the double sum of ⟨xi,xj⟩ over xi in I and xj in J. Terms with nonadjacent distinct vertices vanish. The remaining terms either have both xi and xj in the overlap I∩J, in which case they are exactly the terms appearing in the norm |[I∩J]|, or they come from an adjacency edge connecting I to J with at least one endpoint outside the overlap. Each such boundary adjacency contributes -1, with the double edge between x0 and x1 counted twice, and these are exactly the dangling edges subtracted by δ([I],[J]).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.10
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let [I] be an interval in the C-type lattice C(p,q), and assume I is not the singleton {x0}. Then |[I]|=2+Σxi in I, xi≠x0 (|xi|−2). Equivalently, the norm of a nontrivial interval is obtained by starting with 2 and adding the excess ai−2 over all non-x0 vertices in the interval.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper states this without proof. Directly, if I={xa,...,xb} avoids x0, then |[I]| is the sum of the diagonal weights over I minus twice the number of ordinary edges inside the interval, so |[I]|=Σai−2(b−a)=2+Σ(ai−2). If I contains x0 and at least x1, then the diagonal term 4 from x0 is cancelled by twice the pairing -2 between x0 and x1, while all subsequent internal ordinary edges contribute -2; the same formula remains. This compact formula is used repeatedly to translate the number and size of high-weight vertices in an interval into its norm.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.11 and Remark 3.13
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For any two intervals [I] and [J] in the C-type lattice C(p,q), the dangling-edge count δ([I],[J]) is one of 0,1,2,3. If δ([I],[J])=3, then ⟨x0,[I]⟩=−⟨x0,[J]⟩=±2. More precisely, define two intervals to be distant if they are separated by at least one vertex, to share a common end if they have the same left endpoint or the same right endpoint, to be consecutive if the right endpoint of one is immediately before the left endpoint of the other, to abut if they are consecutive or share a common end, and to satisfy [I] ⋔ [J] if they overlap nontrivially but do not share a common end. If ⟨[I],x0⟩=⟨[J],x0⟩ or one of these two pairings is zero, then δ is 0 for distant intervals, 1 for abutting intervals, and 2 for [I] ⋔ [J]. If ⟨[I],x0⟩ and ⟨[J],x0⟩ are nonzero and unequal, then δ is 2 when the intervals abut and 3 when [I] ⋔ [J]; in this latter unequal-nonzero case the intervals are never distant.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The bound δ≤3 is a combinatorial consequence of the path with one doubled edge: ordinary intervals have at most two boundary adjacencies, and the only way to gain an extra dangling edge is through the doubled x0-x1 edge. The special case δ=3 can only occur when the intervals meet the doubled edge on opposite sides in a way that gives nonzero and opposite pairings with x0. Remark 3.13 refines this by enumerating the possible relative positions of two intervals and whether the doubled edge contributes once or twice to the dangling count.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.14
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the C-type lattice C(p,q), if an interval [A] contains at least two high-weight vertices, meaning at least two vertices xi with i>0 and |xi|>2, then [A] is breakable: it can be written [A]=x+y with |x|≥3, |y|≥3, and ⟨x,y⟩=-1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this as Lemma 3.10 of [BHM+16] and gives no proof. In combination with Proposition 3.7, it supplies the practical criterion that intervals with two or more high-weight vertices are exactly the breakable ones among signed intervals. The intended decomposition cuts the interval between high-weight vertices so that the two pieces have norms at least 3 and meet across a single edge, giving pairing -1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.16
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If two C-type lattices C(p,q) and C(p′,q′), with q>p and q′>p′ in the paper's setting, are isomorphic as lattices, then p=p′ and q=q′. Equivalently, the abstract lattice structure determines the ordered continued-fraction weight sequence (|x1|,|x2|,...,|xn|), and hence determines the prism-manifold parameters p and q.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof reconstructs the vertex weights from irreducible and unbreakable elements. First, except in the special case (p,q)=(2,3), the element ±x0 is characterized intrinsically as the unique signed unbreakable irreducible element y of norm 4 such that ⟨y,v⟩ is even for every lattice element v. Indeed, any other norm-4 unbreakable interval with all even pairings would have to avoid odd pairings with neighboring vertices, forcing it to run to an end of the chain; unbreakability then forces only x1 to be high weight, and a final endpoint-pairing check leaves only the exceptional n=1, |x1|=4 case. Next let R be the sublattice generated by x0 and all norm-2 vectors. Since the lattice has no norm-1 vector, norm-2 vectors are irreducible; by the interval norm formula, R is generated by x0 and exactly those vertex basis elements xi with |xi|=2. Let V0 be the set of unbreakable irreducibles outside ±x0 with norm at least 3, and quotient V0 by the relation v∼u if v−u or v+u lies in R. Each equivalence class corresponds to one high-weight vertex, and the norm of any representative gives that vertex's weight. Let W be the set of indecomposable components of R; these correspond to x0 and to maximal runs of 2's in the weight sequence. Build a bipartite graph B on V∪W, connecting a high-weight class to a run component when representatives pair by -1, and connecting to the x0 component when the representative pairs with x0 by -2. This graph is a path whose distinguished end is the component containing x0. Traversing the path from that end recovers, in order, the runs of 2's and the high weights, hence the sequence (|x1|,...,|xn|). The defining continued fraction then recovers p and q.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 4.3, 4.4, 4.5, 4.6, and 4.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let σ=(σ0,...,σn+1) be a changemaker vector in Z^{n+2}, let e0,...,en+1 be the standard orthonormal basis, and let S={v1,...,vn+1} be Greene's standard basis of the changemaker lattice (σ)⊥. In this basis, vj is tight if σj=1+σ0+...+σj−1 and vj=2e0+e1+...+ej−1−ej; otherwise vj=Σi in A ei−ej for a subset A of {0,...,j−1} representing σj and chosen lexicographically maximally, and vj is just right or gappy according to whether A has a gap. The paper records the following facts from Greene and [BHM+16]: if |vk+1|=2 then k is not a gappy index for any vj; every standard basis element vj is irreducible in (σ)⊥; if vj is breakable then vj is tight; if vt is tight and vj=et+ej−1−ej with j>t, then vt+vj is irreducible; if vt is tight and vt+1=e0+e1+...+et−et+1, then vt+1−vt is irreducible; and for every vj one has j−1 in supp(vj).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: These are cited rather than reproved: Lemma 4.3 is Greene's Lemma 3.12(3), Lemma 4.4 is Greene's Lemma 3.13, Lemma 4.5 is Greene's Lemma 3.15, Lemma 4.6 is Greene's Lemma 3.14(2),(3), and Lemma 4.7 is Lemma 4.9 of [BHM+16]. The common mechanism is the lexicographically maximal choice in the standard basis: gaps in support are restricted by norm-2 basis vectors, each vj has a compulsory last preceding support coordinate j−1, and tight vectors are the only standard basis vectors with enough support to break. These facts are later combined with the interval classification in the C-type lattice to compare the changemaker standard basis to the C-type vertex basis.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In a C-type lattice C(p,q), there is no element v with |v|=2 and ⟨v,x0⟩≠0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Corollary 3.4, C(p,q) is indecomposable, so it contains no norm-1 vector: a norm-1 vector in an integral positive definite lattice would split off a rank-one orthogonal summand. If v had norm 2 and were reducible, v=a+b with a,b nonzero and ⟨a,b⟩≥0; positive definiteness would force |a|=|b|=1 and ⟨a,b⟩=0, impossible without norm-1 vectors. Thus v is irreducible. Proposition 3.2 then gives v=±[I] for an interval. Lemma 3.10 implies a norm-2 interval contains only x0 or norm-2 non-x0 vertices. It cannot contain x1 because a1≥3, and it cannot contain x0 unless it is the singleton x0, whose norm is 4. Hence the interval is disjoint from x0 and x1, so its pairing with x0 is zero.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.9 and Corollary 4.10
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume a C-type lattice C(p,q) is isomorphic to a changemaker lattice (σ)⊥ inside Z^{n+2}, with σ0=1, and let x0 be the distinguished norm-4 vertex-basis element of C(p,q). Then, after possibly changing the global sign of the isomorphism, there exist indices k1<k2<k3 such that x0 is either e0+ek1+ek2−ek3 or e0−ek1−ek2+ek3. Moreover, the first standard basis vector v1 is 2e0−e1 if k1>1 and is e0−e1 if k1=1; in the sign pattern x0=e0−ek1−ek2+ek3 the first case k1>1 must occur.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: A norm-4 vector in the ambient orthonormal lattice is either ±2ek or a signed sum of four distinct unit vectors. The first type cannot lie in (σ)⊥ because all σi are positive once σ0=1. Write x0=δ0ek0+δ1ek1+δ2ek2+δ3ek3 with k0<k1<k2<k3. After a global sign change take δ0=1. If k0>0, then x0 pairs oddly with vk0, contradicting Lemma 3.5, so k0=0. If σki=σkj but δi≠δj, then δieki+δjekj is a norm-2 vector in (σ)⊥ pairing nontrivially with x0, contradicting Lemma 4.8; hence equal σ-coordinates must have equal signs. The equation ⟨x0,σ⟩=0 and the monotonicity of σ then restrict the signs: if δ1=-1, one must have δ2=-1 and δ3=1; if δ1=1, one must have δ3=-1 and δ2=1, since δ2=-1 would force σ0+σk1=σk2+σk3 and hence equality patterns forbidden by the previous observation. Corollary 4.10 follows because v1 is either e0−e1 or 2e0−e1, and Lemma 3.5 plus Lemma 4.8 exclude the wrong parity or norm-2 pairing with x0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.13 and Corollaries 4.14, 4.15, 4.16
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Continue in the setting where C(p,q)≅(σ)⊥, with standard changemaker basis S={v1,...,vn+1}, and replace vk3 by x0 to form S′=(S−{vk3})∪{x0}. Each vi is irreducible, so in C(p,q) write vi=εi[vi] for a signed interval. If vi and vj are unbreakable standard basis vectors with i,j≠k3, then it cannot happen that [vi] contains x0 while [vj] contains x1 but not x0; in particular δ([vi],[vj])≤2. Consequently, if vi and vj are distinct unbreakable standard basis vectors with i,j≠k3, then |⟨vi,vj⟩|≤1, and equality can occur only when their intervals abut. If vi and vj are unbreakable, have norms at least 3, are not indexed by k3, and are distinct, then their unique high-weight vertices xzi and xzj are distinct. Finally, among unbreakable standard basis vectors vj with j≠k3, at most one has ⟨vj,x0⟩ nonzero.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper first proves Lemma 4.12: for j≠k3, ⟨vj,x0⟩≥0. This is checked from the two possible sign patterns for x0 in Proposition 4.9; in the harder sign pattern, a hypothetical value -2 would allow replacing supp+(vj) by a lexicographically larger subset, contradicting the definition of the standard basis. For Lemma 4.13, suppose [vi] contains x0 and [vj] contains x1 but not x0. Since i,j≠k3, Lemma 4.12 forces both pairings with x0 to be +2, fixing the signs and left endpoints of the intervals. Unbreakability and a1≥3 imply both intervals have the same unique high-weight vertex x1. The interval-pairing formula then gives ⟨vi,vj⟩=-|vi|+δ, so this pairing is nonpositive and very restricted. An explicit support analysis using Proposition 4.9 shows in every possible sign pattern for x0 that the standard-basis supports force ⟨vi,vj⟩≥1, a contradiction. Corollary 4.14 reduces all remaining configurations either to the known linear-lattice case from Greene or to the just-excluded configuration, using the reflection in x0 when needed. Corollary 4.15 follows because if two such unbreakable intervals shared the same high-weight vertex, Lemmas 3.9 and 3.10 would force their pairing to be at least 1; Corollary 4.14 would then force them to abut, but abutting gives δ=1 and the same calculation yields a pairing too large. Corollary 4.16 follows because a nonzero pairing with x0 for an unbreakable interval forces the unique high-weight vertex to be x1, and Corollary 4.15 allows this for at most one vector.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.19, Lemma 4.21, Lemma 4.23, Lemma 4.24, and Corollary 4.25
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the C-type/changemaker comparison setting, let T be a set of irreducible elements of C(p,q), each represented by a signed interval. The intersection graph G(T) has vertex set T and an edge when the corresponding intervals abut; the pairing graph Ĝ(T) has an edge when the lattice pairing is nonzero. For T⊂S′, Proposition 4.19 says G(T) is obtained from Ĝ(T) by deleting only some edges incident to breakable vectors; in particular, on the subset of unbreakable elements of S′, the two graphs agree. Lemma 4.21 says G(T) has no claw, i.e. no vertex adjacent to three pairwise nonadjacent vertices. Lemma 4.23 says the graph G of the unbreakable part of S′ has no heavy triple: there do not exist three vertices of norm at least 3, none equal to ±x0, such that each pair is connected by a path disjoint from the third. Lemma 4.24 says that if the elements of T are linearly independent, then every cycle in G(T) induces a complete subgraph. Corollary 4.25 says every cycle in G of the unbreakable part of S′ has length three.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Lemma 4.18 first observes that abutting intervals always have nonzero pairing: if one interval is x0 the pairing is ±2, and otherwise Lemma 3.9 gives a nonzero contribution because δ=1 in an abutting configuration. Proposition 4.19 combines this with Corollary 4.14, which says that for unbreakable standard-basis intervals nonzero pairing can only occur through abutment; hence any discrepancy between the pairing and intersection graphs must involve a breakable vector. Lemma 4.21 is cited from Greene's Lemma 4.8 and is a combinatorial property of interval-abutment graphs. Lemma 4.23 uses Corollary 4.15: three unbreakable norm-at-least-3 intervals have distinct high-weight vertices, so order them along the path as xzi<xzj<xzk. Any path in the intersection graph from the left interval to the right interval must pass through an interval containing the middle high-weight vertex, and Corollary 4.15 forces that interval to be the middle chosen vertex itself; hence the path cannot be disjoint from it. Lemma 4.24 is cited as identical to Greene's Lemma 3.8. For Corollary 4.25, Corollary 4.16 prevents x0 from lying on a cycle in the unbreakable graph. A cycle is complete by Lemma 4.24. It cannot contain three vertices of norm greater than 2 by Lemma 4.23. If it contained two norm-2 vertices, the standard-basis support restrictions from Lemma 4.3 imply no third vector can pair nontrivially with both, contradicting completeness. Therefore the only possible cycles are triangles.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.26
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the C-type/changemaker setting, let m<N be positive integers with k3 not in the interval [m,N]. Suppose vm is unbreakable and neighbors either x0 or some unbreakable vj with j<m in the intersection graph. Suppose also that for every i with m<i≤N, the standard basis vector vi is unbreakable and min supp(vi)≥m. Then |vi|=2 for every i with m<i≤N.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is by induction on i. For i=m+1, Lemma 4.7 gives m in supp(vm+1), and min supp(vm+1)≥m forces vm+1=em−em+1, so its norm is 2. Assume |vi|=2 for m<i<l≤N. Let t=min supp(vl). By Lemmas 4.3 and 4.7, the vector vl is just right relative to the norm-2 string already constructed. If m<t<l−1, then the intersection graph has a claw centered at vt with leaves vl, vt−1, and vt+1. If t=m and vm neighbors x0, then vm is the center of a claw with leaves vl, x0, and vm+1; if t=m and vm neighbors an earlier unbreakable vj, then vm is the center of a claw with leaves vl, vj, and vm+1. Lemma 4.21 forbids all these cases. Thus t=l−1, which forces vl=el−1−el and |vl|=2.

proof_techniques.name: Classify irreducibles as signed interval sums
proof_techniques.description: The paper's C-type lattice is path-like, and Proposition 3.2 allows every irreducible element to be treated as a signed sum of consecutive vertex-basis elements. This replaces arbitrary lattice decompositions with combinatorics of intervals: endpoints, overlaps, containment, consecutive intervals, and boundary edges. Once irreducibles are intervals, norm and pairing computations become finite graph calculations rather than coefficient-by-coefficient algebra.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a general weighted tree, the analogous object to try is a signed sum over a connected subtree. A proof of the original problem might attempt to show that if a vertex is reducible, its decomposing pieces force connected-subtree behavior, and then use the bad-vertex condition w(v)<d(v) to contradict positive definiteness or to isolate an irreducible vertex.

proof_techniques.name: Overlap-minus-dangling-edges formula
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 3.9 computes the pairing of two interval sums as the norm of their overlap minus the number of dangling edges between them. This packages all diagonal contributions and all adjacency contributions into a single graph-theoretic identity. Lemmas 3.10 and 3.11 then make the formula effective: interval norms are controlled by excess weights |xi|−2, and the dangling-edge count is bounded by the interval geometry.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For any weighted tree lattice, a similar formula holds for sums over connected vertex sets: the inner product is the weighted overlap contribution minus boundary adjacencies, with internal edges counted in the norm. This can be used to quantify how a proposed reducible decomposition interacts with the tree's edges and with a vertex whose weight is smaller than its degree.

proof_techniques.name: Use irreducible basis elements to prove indecomposability
proof_techniques.description: Corollary 3.4 uses a general lattice principle: if an irreducible element is projected to an orthogonal direct sum, one projection must vanish, since otherwise the element is decomposed into two nonzero orthogonal summands with nonnegative pairing. Therefore irreducible basis elements must lie wholly in one summand. If the pairing graph of those basis elements is connected, all basis elements lie in the same summand, proving indecomposability.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If one can establish irreducibility for a subset of vertices or for strategically chosen combinations of vertices, connectedness of the tree can force global restrictions. Conversely, assuming all vertices reducible may create a decomposition or projection structure incompatible with the connected tree.

proof_techniques.name: Detect breakability through high-weight vertices
proof_techniques.description: The paper defines a stronger notion than reducibility: an interval is breakable if it splits into two norm-at-least-3 pieces pairing -1. Proposition 3.7 and Lemma 3.14 show that, in the C-type lattice, breakability of signed intervals is exactly controlled by whether the interval contains at least two high-weight vertices, i.e. vertices of weight greater than the path baseline 2. This allows the authors to separate unbreakable intervals, which behave like single high-weight blocks, from breakable intervals, which can be cut at an edge between high-weight vertices.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem involves a vertex whose weight is small relative to degree, so an analogous baseline might be degree rather than 2. A useful adaptation would be to measure a connected subtree by the excess Σ(w(x)−d_subtree_baseline(x)) and show that too many high-excess regions force a controlled splitting, leaving some vertex or small subtree irreducible.

proof_techniques.name: Parity and reflection at a distinguished vertex
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 3.5 exploits the special C-type feature that x0 pairs evenly with every lattice element. This makes the reflection across x0⊥ an integral lattice automorphism. The paper uses this reflection to reduce intervals containing x0 to intervals not containing x0 and to transfer known linear-lattice arguments to the C-type setting.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the weighted tree has a vertex whose pairings impose congruence restrictions, an integral reflection or parity argument may reduce local configurations. Even when no such reflection exists globally, checking pairings modulo small integers can rule out decompositions of low-norm vertices.

proof_techniques.name: Rule out low-norm interactions using absence of norm-one vectors
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 4.8 shows that a norm-2 element in an indecomposable positive definite integral lattice with no norm-1 vector must be irreducible. In the C-type lattice, this reduces a hypothetical norm-2 vector pairing with x0 to a signed interval; the interval norm formula then forces it away from x0, contradiction. This is a compact way of turning positive definiteness and indecomposability into restrictions on possible pairings.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Positive definite tree lattices often have no norm-one vector under natural weight assumptions. If a vertex has small norm or if a reducible decomposition would create norm-one or norm-two pieces, this technique can eliminate the decomposition.

proof_techniques.name: Compare bases via pairing and intersection graphs
proof_techniques.description: In Section 4, the authors compare the changemaker standard basis to the C-type vertex basis by representing standard basis elements as signed intervals. They define an intersection graph using interval abutment and compare it to the pairing graph. For unbreakable elements, these graphs coincide, and the graph is constrained by no-claw, no-heavy-triple, and triangle-only-cycle rules.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a proof of the original problem proceeds by assuming every vertex is reducible and replacing vertices by decomposing elements, graph constraints on how irreducible or minimal pieces can pair may be useful. The no-claw style arguments are especially suggestive for trees, where high-degree branching must be reconciled with interval- or subtree-like irreducible pieces.

proof_techniques.name: Inductive forcing of norm-two strings
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 4.26 is a template for proving that a long run of standard-basis elements must all have norm 2. The induction uses support constraints, the fact that each standard basis vector contains the immediately preceding coordinate, and graph obstructions: any attempt to start support too early creates a forbidden claw. Thus local adjacency plus unbreakability forces a rigid chain of norm-2 vectors.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a weighted tree, a similar induction could show that certain branches forced by reducibility must consist of minimal-weight vertices. A bad vertex with weight below degree may then be incompatible with all incident branches being minimal in the required way.

proof_techniques.name: Recover the weighted graph from intrinsic irreducible data
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 3.16 reconstructs the ordered C-type weight sequence from the abstract lattice by identifying x0, norm-2 runs, and high-weight vertices using irreducible and unbreakable elements. It then builds a bipartite path whose vertices are high-weight classes and norm-2 components, and traversal of this path recovers the original vertex basis weights.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This suggests a robust strategy: define intrinsic classes of irreducible or minimally reducible elements, then recover enough of the tree's combinatorics to force one original vertex to appear among them. Such reconstruction ideas may be helpful if the original problem is attacked by contradiction from a different basis.

other_useful_info: Section 3's main irreducible-element classification is imported from Greene [Gre13, Proposition 3.3], and its unbreakability criterion uses Greene [Gre13, Corollary 3.5(4)] and [BHM+16, Lemma 3.10]; those cited results may contain broader linear-plumbing arguments useful beyond the C-type case. The introduction explicitly states that the vertex basis elements of a C-type lattice are irreducible, and Corollary 3.4 uses this fact, although the paper does not isolate it as a numbered lemma. The C-type lattice is not an arbitrary tree lattice: it is a path-like positive definite lattice with one doubled edge between x0 and x1, so direct transfer to the original problem likely requires replacing intervals by connected subtrees and replacing the baseline weight 2 by a degree-sensitive quantity.
why_relevant: Continues the changemaker-lattice analysis for C-type plumbing lattices and uses irreducible elements to compare standard and vertex bases. Its methods are relevant for proving that a weighted-tree basis must contain irreducible elements.
